text: Egyptian theology of divine kingship—understanding a king as simultaneously human and divine—provides institutional-theological precedent for "Son of God" as political designation rather than metaphysical claim.
quote: Egyptians grappled with this arguably more than anybody else because the king is human and here on Earth and yet is meant to be The Offspring of divinity right so how do you have human being on Earth who is divine who will still die... Jesus is a part of that discussion he is part Divine he is part human he is Mortal he can be killed he can be reborn

text: Luke edits Mark's passion narrative, reshaping Jesus's death as philosophically controlled and Socratic, demonstrating how Gospel authors deliberately adapted narratives to fit Greek heroic and philosophical models that would appeal to Greek audiences—direct evidence of the Greco-Christian framework shaping early Christian narrative.
quote: luke takes the marking passion narrative takes everything embarrassing out of it except the fact of crucifixion and turns it into a very legible story of a hero's death a story that resonated with anyone who loved achilles or loved socrates
